titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary Davidson, 19 February 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother. James writes about waiting for deliveries of parcels and his concern that his letters home are not being delivered. As per his previous letter, he stresses his need for replacement boots and requests that any items of kit be sent as soon as possible. He also mentions the conditions of the trenches and considerable shelling and fighting in nearby sections.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
19/2/16 My dearest Mother â I got your
letter of 13th yest & was surprised
to hear that you had not received
any letters from me since
1st. I wrote on 1st & 8th then to
Father on 10th & again to you
on 16th. I do hope you have
since received them as I am
now very badly off for boots
& looking anxiously every day
for those trench books to turn
up. The boots I had have
simply gone to pieces with the
trench work & my feet are
always wet. With regard to
the pair of boots which you say
have arrived from Manfields
I cant remember whether I
ordered them or not but at
any rate they have come just
at the right time & you might
please have them also sent on
as soon as possible. I
think I asked you to post me
a box of Cockles pills â these have
not come yet so if by chance
they have been overlooked would
you please get them for me.
There was a terrific bombardment
this evening to the left of our
Sector â it was like continuous
thunder & the flashes of the guns
lit up the whole sky. Our
particular part of the the line has
been rather more peaceful during
the past day or so but one of
my gun teams had a great
escape. We had been annoying
the Germans considerably the
night before with 2 guns so
they started to look for us
with artillery & actually dropped
2 shells right on top of the
emplacement where the men
were taking shelter. Fortunately
it had a concrete roof & the
shell did not penetrate for
otherwise everyone would have
been killed & they were all
men of my own original section.
Am kept awfully busy â never
seem to have a minute â but
have been keeping all right in
spite of the weather. Trenches
have been flooded & yest I
was wading all morning up to nearly
the knees & with my ordinary old
boots so you can imagine what
a state I was in. Must be off
to bed. With fondest love to all. Your loving son, Jim A Zepp as just been passing over
but too high to see
On Active Service
24th/16 Mrs. S.C.Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co Down Ireland JDavidson 19/2/16
